text.content:
Three routes east. The harbor at sunset and the choice of which deck to stand on.

Once the party has named the broker (Tarsh), resolved Tamsin's fate, recovered the indictment package against Cindren & Vhal, and understood the harbor-before-throne timing, they prepare to leave Hardby for Rel Astra. Three routes are available; the choice this time is shaped less by political cover (which they have or do not have based on Phase 2's choice) and more by speed-versus-stealth.

Route 1 — By Sea, the Coastal Galley

A small Hardby-flagged coastal galley named the Lacquer Wave sails the regular weekly run from Hardby's outer dock to Rel Astra's Old Harbor with stops at Saltmarsh and a half-dozen smaller ports. Six days under sail in good weather, eight to ten in bad. The captain, Yorra Mel, is a Hardby woman with no firm ties and no questions; she takes paying passengers and asks for half the fare in advance.

What it costs

  • Speed. Slow — six to ten days. Slower than the overland direct.
  • Visibility. Some. The Lacquer Wave is a known coastal vessel; passenger lists go to Hardby's outer harbor authority, who in turn share them with Rel Astra's harbor commission as a courtesy. Cindren & Vhal Rel Astra will know the party arrived by galley within hours of disembarkation.
  • Cost. Eight gold per passenger, full fare. Cabins shared.

What it buys

  • The party is unreachable while at sea. No assassinations, no political pressure, no couriers from anyone. Six days of forced rest in which to read everything they recovered, write to Caelith from a port, and plan their Rel Astra approach.
  • Smuggling room. The galley's cargo hold is mostly wool bales and salt cod. The party's recovered evidence package can be folded into a wool bale and forgotten; it will not be searched.
  • A Rel Astra arrival in the Old Harbor, not the formal Customs Quay. The Old Harbor is the same district where Cindren & Vhal Rel Astra has its main counting house. The party can begin Phase 4 on the conspiracy's doorstep.

Pressure Clock interaction

This route does not advance the Phase 3 Pressure Clock at departure. It does, however, mean that the party's arrival in Rel Astra is announced to the conspiracy roughly 12 hours before they can do anything about it.

Route 2 — By Land, the Old Coastal Road

The overland coastal road from Hardby to Rel Astra runs east through Saltmarsh territory, around the southern end of the Wild Coast, and inland through the Aerdi southern provinces. Five days hard riding, seven at a normal pace. Open road, well-traveled, generally safe — but the conspiracy can intercept if it knows to look.

What it costs

  • Speed. Faster than the galley but exposed. Five days minimum.
  • Visibility. Variable. The first day out of Hardby is the dangerous one; after that, the party blends into the road's normal traffic. If the firm has had Quiet Pursuit-level surprise (i.e. the party is leaving fast, ahead of suspicion), the road is safe. If the firm has had Commercial Cover-level alarm time, expect a single hired-blade encounter on day three. If the firm has had Official Delegation-level visibility, expect to be shadowed the whole way and a serious assassination attempt on day four — though the diplomatic escort is also a deterrent.
  • Cost. Two gold per horse hired or three gold per remount; the party may already have horses from Phase 2.

What it buys

  • Speed. The fastest viable route by a comfortable margin if all goes well.
  • Optionality. The road passes within a day of the Whitemoor Estate (where Tamsin was held on Commercial Cover) and within half a day of three other coastal estates the party may want to investigate later.
  • Direct entry to Rel Astra by the West Gate, not the harbor. Less initially observed by the Old Harbor Ward.

Pressure Clock interaction

This route advances the Pressure Clock by 1 segment per day on the road for Commercial Cover and Official Delegation parties. Quiet Pursuit parties are not affected unless they are recognized.

Route 3 — By Diplomatic Carriage (Official Delegation only)

The accredited Yeomanry delegation continues east in formal procession, with the same councilman, clerks, and guard escort. Eight to ten days, with formal stops at Aerdi customs posts and a brief audience requested at the Aerdy southern province governor's seat. Slow, formal, immune to interception by anyone who values their head.

What it costs

  • Speed. The slowest route. Eight to ten days, and the party is not in control of the schedule — the councilman is.
  • Visibility. Total. Every Aerdi authority east of Hardby will know the delegation is coming, where it is at any given moment, and roughly what it intends. The conspiracy will know everything it needs to prepare for the party's Rel Astra arrival.

What it buys

  • Absolute legal protection. The party arrives in Rel Astra with full diplomatic accreditation, formal audiences pre-arranged with the harbor commission and the Old Harbor Ward Council, and immunity from arrest or detention for the duration of the delegation.
  • Audience with Tarlith Vhal Sereth. The delegation's standing means the party can request, and receive, a formal interview with Sereth at his offices. This does not happen on any other route. Whether the interview yields anything is a Phase 4 question; the access itself is the value.
  • The court-issued warrant from Hardby (Path 3) executes cleanly at Rel Astra's Old Harbor Ward, with Aerdi watch cooperation. Without the diplomatic standing, the warrant is paper.

Pressure Clock interaction

This route advances the Pressure Clock by 2 segments at departure (the formal procession is impossible to hide) and by 1 segment per three days en route. By the time the delegation reaches Rel Astra, the conspiracy has had every opportunity to prepare. It does not matter as much as on the other routes — the legal protections are themselves the protection — but Phase 4 starts under maximum political weight.

Caelith's framing of the choice

By sealed letter (Letter 3 of his continuing correspondence; see Arrival → Caelith's Continuing Letters), Caelith has already given the party his thinking. The short version, paraphrased here for ease of reference:

"If you are carrying the smoking-gun document — the one that names Sereth — send it to me by separate courier and travel with a copy. The original is safer in Trina's strongroom than in your saddlebags east of Hardby.

If you are quick and lightly accompanied, take the road. If you are heavy with witnesses or evidence, take the galley. If you are still under the councilman's accreditation and you intend to use the warrant publicly in Rel Astra, take the carriage and accept the time cost.

Whichever you choose, write to me from your last port or post-house before Rel Astra. I will know whether to send a follow-up courier, and where."

Hardby's pressure clock is faster than Loftwick's. Hardby is a freer city, less restrained, and the conspiracy is operationally based here — they can act through hired hands and corrupt watchmen in ways they could not in Loftwick. Six segments, same shape as Phase 2's clock, but the threats are physical rather than political.

The clock at a glance — six escalating rhythms

  1. Recognition — someone in the city realizes who the party is.
  2. Surveillance — the firm puts watchers on the party's lodging.
  3. Witness loss — Vella, Hesren, Lerra, Veska, or Hella vanishes or is killed (GM picks based on which threads the party has activated).
  4. Hired blade — a single contracted attempt on a party member, not the whole group, in a public place.
  5. Squad attack — a coordinated mercenary assault on the party's lodging or on a single PC walking alone.
  6. The harbor closes — the firm pays the harbor watch to delay or seize ships and post-horses; the party's exit becomes a problem.

Triggers — what advances the clock

TriggerSegments
The party is recognized in Hardby (Quiet Pursuit only — see Arrival)+2
The party visits the Cindren & Vhal Hardby branch openly more than twice (Commercial Cover)+1
The Cindren & Vhal branch burglary succeeds+3 immediate
The Cindren & Vhal branch burglary is detected (Marsh wakes early, items missed)+1 additional
The party confronts Solen Mereth without a procedurally clean evidence package+2
The party confronts Tarsh in the Coopered Wreck and combat breaks out+1
The party kills Tarsh, Solen, or Mira Cindren+2 (firm assumes a third party in play; see Tarsh dossier)
Tamsin Moraven recovery raid (Black Cog Lane warehouse, Quiet Pursuit)+2
Tamsin Moraven recovery raid (Whitemoor Estate, Commercial Cover)+2
Subpoena filed against C&V Hardby (Official Delegation)+1
Each full session day in which the party makes no investigative progress in Hardby+1

Milestones — what happens at each segment

Segment 1 — Recognition

A face the party did not notice notices them. Could be a former C&V courier in a tavern, a watchman who was once on Reln Pollow's payroll back in Loftwick and has retired east, a Hardby waterfront fence who knew Sarth's name. The recognition is private and quiet; the recognizer reports to Solen Mereth or to Tarsh by morning. The firm now knows the party is in Hardby, even if they don't yet know what the party is doing.

Segment 2 — Surveillance

Two watchers are placed on the party's lodging — Castrian's house, the inn they took, the safe-house Ailen offered. The watchers are competent and uninteresting-looking; they note comings and goings and report to a runner three times daily. Perception DC 13 to spot one watcher; DC 16 to confirm the second. The party can make and break surveillance contact for game purposes (lose the watchers temporarily by going through Castrian's back garden, etc.) but the firm's intelligence picture sharpens with every additional day in the city.

Segment 3 — Witness loss

One of the party's contacts is taken or killed. GM picks based on which threads the party has activated and what would hurt most:

  • Vella Tannin — the sympathetic clerk. If the party has approached her and not yet extracted her, she vanishes between work and her lodging. Her body is found two days later in a barrel in the canal.
  • Hesren Vesh — if the party has approached him and not yet extracted him, he is "reassigned" to a coastal posting; the carriage taking him there does not arrive at its destination.
  • Lerra Voss — if the party recovered Tamsin's body or letter through her. She is taken on a quiet street.
  • Hella Voren — if Veska is gone and Hella is the party's primary contact. She is dismissed from the Quay clearing-house on a fabricated charge of misfiling.
  • Castrian Vell — if the party has nothing else to lose. His house is broken into; he is roughed up but not killed; he leaves Hardby for Saltmarsh within a week.

Segment 4 — Hired blade

A single contracted attempt on a single party member in a public place. A passing knife in a market crowd, a weighted purse swung at the back of the head outside a tavern, a single crossbow shot from a rooftop. The contractor is anonymous; if pursued, the trail goes cold within three streets. The attempt is meant to be survivable but to make the party's continuing presence in Hardby uncomfortable.

Segment 5 — Squad attack

A coordinated assault on the party's lodging or on a single PC walking alone. Five to seven hired blades (a Bandit Captain leading Bandits and Thugs), well-armed, professionally led. This is a real combat encounter and should be tuned for the party's level. The squad is not trying to kill all of the party — they are trying to recover specific items (the indictment package) and kill specific people (Tamsin, if she is with the party; the PC most likely to be carrying the documents). They will withdraw if the items are recovered or if they take more than 50% casualties.

Segment 6 — The harbor closes

The firm pays the harbor watch to delay shipping and post-horses. The Lacquer Wave's next sailing is "cancelled for repairs." The post-stable refuses to rent horses without seven days' written notice. Coastal galleys leave only with passenger lists pre-approved by the harbor commission, which is now slow-walking everything. The party's exit becomes a problem they must solve creatively — bribing a fishing skiff, hiring private mounts at triple price, calling on Castrian's wine-merchant friend to provide a private carriage, or simply leaving on foot.

Phase 4 begins under whatever exit conditions the party manages to engineer.

How to use the clock at the table

  • Show the players the clock face. Six segments, visible.
  • When you advance, say which trigger fired.
  • Phase 3's clock is faster than Phase 2's because Hardby is faster than Loftwick. Don't over-protect the party from segment 4 and 5 — these scenes are meant to happen, and they should land with appropriate stakes.
  • The clock should reach 4–5 segments by the time the party leaves Hardby. That is the intended outcome. Phase 4 begins with Cindren & Vhal Rel Astra fully aware that the Yeomanry hammer is on its way.

pages.name: Ending Read-Aloud — Departing for Rel Astra

text.content:
Read aloud at the moment the party leaves Hardby for Rel Astra. The text below is route-agnostic; adjust the small details (galley deck vs. road vs. carriage) to match the choice the party made.

Hardby in your last hour is the same as Hardby in your first: coal smoke and salt and the sound of a clock-tower bell that runs five seconds slow against every other clock you have heard since Loftwick. Whoever first set Hardby's main bell, three centuries ago, set it slightly wrong and no one has ever bothered to fix it. The city has run by it ever since.

You carry, in a pouch sewn into a wool bale or strapped under a saddle skirt or folded inside the lining of a diplomatic dispatch case, a copied indictment package: Veska Maelan's notarized opinion, three pages of carbon-copied second-set ledger entries (or, if you took the originals, the bound volumes themselves), Tarlith Vhal Sereth's signature impression rubbed up from the senior partner's blotter, and the chipped Hardby clearing-stamp that officially does not exist. The original of Veska's opinion is on its way back to Loftwick by separate courier; what you carry is your proof on the road.

You also carry — depending on what you accomplished — a living woman whose mother fed her wheat-water in the warehouse, or a small folded letter in her hand that you will need to put into her husband's hands when you next see him; an extracted clerk who has agreed to testify; a name (Anver Resh) who has agreed not to obstruct, in exchange for never appearing in your indictment.

You also carry, more quietly, what you have learned. The conspiracy is not Cindren and Vhal. The conspiracy is positioning to seat three commissioners on Rel Astra's harbor before the next Aerdy Overking is named, so that whoever wins the eastern succession finds Cindren-aligned hands controlling the eastern revenue. There are three vacant seats. There are three candidates. You do not yet know who the candidates are.

You do not yet know the name behind the single capital letter "E." You do not yet know whose hand wrote the only annotation Veska could not place. You do not yet know what is older than the conspiracy you have named.

Rel Astra rises out of the morning haze on the eastern shore, six days after Hardby for the slow routes and three for the road. The city is older than Hardby, larger than Loftwick, and runs on a clock that nobody set wrong: the harbor's, which keeps Aerdi imperial time exactly. Tarlith Vhal Sereth keeps an office on the Goldsmiths' Quarter side of the Old Harbor Ward, in a four-story building you have never seen and have, for two phases now, been preparing yourselves to walk into.

You arrive on the morning of the third week.

What the party leaves Phase 3 with

  • The names: Solen Mereth, Tarlith Vhal Sereth, Anver "Tarsh" Resh.
  • The indictment package — Veska's notarial opinion plus the recovered branch records (whether by burglary, copy-reading, or subpoena) plus the Sereth blotter rubbing plus the defect-stamp.
  • Tamsin's outcome resolved — alive (rescued from Hardby or Whitemoor) or dead (with body recovered and last letter in hand).
  • Hesren Vesh extracted or lost (depending on whether the party found him in time and on which travel approach made it possible).
  • The harbor-before-throne reading clarified. Three vacant seats on Rel Astra's harbor commission. Three candidates the conspiracy intends to seat before the next Overking is named. Identity of those candidates: unknown. Identity of the Aerdy claimant the conspiracy supports: unknown.
  • The fourth-hand "E." observation from Veska. Three small details about how the letter is formed. Phase 3 does not name Eclavdra. Phase 3 does not even know to look for her.

Where Phase 4 picks up

Phase 4 begins with the party's arrival in Rel Astra — the city the conspiracy actually operates from, the city where Tarlith Vhal Sereth keeps his offices, the city whose harbor commission is the conspiracy's prize. The Phase 4 questions are:

  • Who are the three candidate commissioners the conspiracy intends to seat?
  • Which Aerdy claimant do the conspirators support — or do they support none, intending instead to control the eastern revenue regardless of who wins?
  • Can Tarlith Vhal Sereth be brought to indictment in Aerdi court, or only in Yeomanry court via written deposition?
  • What is the older shape of the conspiracy that "E." sits behind?

Phase 4 may name "E." (it does not have to). Phase 4 may name the Aerdy claimant (it should). Phase 4 must, before its end, either bring Tarlith Vhal Sereth to court or remove him from play.

Phase 5, if there is one, is whatever is older than this.
